# GSAP

Best selection of **GSAP Website** examples for your inspiration...Read more

Performance is paramount, especially on mobile devices with sluggish processors. Silky smooth HTML5 javascript animation is the hallmark of any tweening engine worth its weight and GSAP outperforms old “industry standards” by literally 1000%+ under stress.

GSAP is packed with features that make most other engines look like cheap toys. Animate colors, beziers, css properties, arrays, scrolls and lots more. Round values, smoothly reverse() on the fly, use relative values, automatically accommodate getter/setter functions, employ virtually any easing equation, and manage conflicting tweens like a pro. Define callbacks, tween in seconds or frames, build sequences effortlessly (even with overlapping tweens), repeat/yoyo and more.

Learn more about it here: [**GSAP**](https://greensock.com/gsap)

# Scrolling

Best selection of **Scrolling Website** examples for your inspiration...Read more

In computers, scrolling is the action of sliding text, images or video across a monitor or display, generally by using a scroll well. Scrolling does not change the layout of the text or pictures, but incrementally moves the user's view across the screen. Smooth scrolling is a feature that reduces what the viewer would perceive as jumps (discontinuous movement) in the display. When this feature is applied to touchscreens, it is referred to as kinetic scrolling. .

Scrolling can be both horizontal and vertical. Vertical scrolling is the most common one, normally, web browsers place as many words in a single line as will fit the width of the browser window and when the text surpasses the screen size, vertical scrolling is required to continue reading. Horizontal scrolling works well with portfolios and gallery websites, but not so much with text-driven sites.

![Thumbnail (1920x1080)](https://i.ytimg.com/vi/nlm37S3PKG4/maxresdefault.jpg)
# [Awwwards-Level List Reveal Animation with GSAP ScrollTrigger | Cinematic View](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nlm37S3PKG4)

**Visibility**: Public
**Uploaded by**: [HankTheTank](http://www.youtube.com/@HankTheTankCoding)
**Uploaded at**: 2026-01-04T04:01:33-08:00
**Published at**: 2026-01-04T04:01:33-08:00
**Length**: 13:02
**Views**: 755
**Likes**: 53
**Category**: Science & Technology

## Description

```
Learn how to build a premium scroll-based list reveal animation with synchronized image previews! This tutorial covers advanced GSAP ScrollTrigger techniques to create smooth, cinematic list animations that respond to scroll position.

🎯 What You'll Learn:
Building a scroll-driven list animation with pinning
Synchronizing image changes with scroll position
Creating smooth transitions with Lenis smooth scroll
Implementing active state animations
Calculating dynamic scroll distances
Making responsive list animations for mobile

💻 Technologies Used:
HTML5 & CSS3
GSAP (GreenSock Animation Platform)
ScrollTrigger Plugin
Lenis Smooth Scroll
Vanilla JavaScript

✨ Perfect for:
Character galleries
Product showcases
Team member portfolios
Case study navigation
Movie/game character lists
Premium landing pages

📚 Key Techniques:
ScrollTrigger pinning
Dynamic scroll calculations
Progress-based animations
onUpdate callbacks
State management with scroll
Image switching on scroll


GSAP: https://greensock.com/gsap/
ScrollTrigger: https://greensock.com/scrolltrigger/
Lenis: https://lenis.studiofreight.com/
Source Code: https://github.com/Hank-D-Tank/rdr2-list/
Live Link: https://dimaac.com/projects/rdr2-list

💡 This effect creates an immersive, interactive experience perfect for showcasing any type of content with style!
Like, subscribe, and hit the bell for more Awwwards-level web animation tutorials!
#gsap #scrolltrigger #webanimation #webdesign #frontend
```

## Transcript

Hey, what's going on guys? Welcome back to the channel. So, in this video, I'm going to be showing you or teaching you guys this cool effect that you might have seen on bunch of awards winning site. When on the left, we have some list items with names of characters from Red Dead Redemption 2, which is one of my uh favorite games. And then on the right, we have the character image. Now, as I scroll through the site, you can see that the active item changes as well as the image changes to that specific active um item and then when you reach all the way end um it just uh fades and then heist the image as well as the list item also is no more active. Now, if you revisit you can basically scheme through the animation again. So yeah, this is a very cool and very fun way of showcasing list items in your website. So yeah, this is what we're going to be building in this video. So without a delay, let's get started and let's learn to build this. Now before starting um this the source code as well as the live link is in the description so you guys can go and check it out and yeah, so let's start. So here I am in my index.html file wherein I have bunch of stuff. Um I have some HTML as well as CSS. Now I'll be explaining you guys in a bit what we have but I'm just going to be first showing you how it renders. So here we have one list item and then the preview image. So for the HTML as you can see we have an image with a class of preview image which has um the first character is image. Then the container with list wrapper which has the list with actual list item with index as well as the name of the character. Now here the tricky part is each of the individual list item would have the data attribute of image. So in JavaScript as the as we scroll and then the list item that is active, we're going to grab onto the data image from that and then we're going to be just swapping it with um we basically will take that and then we'll replace this uh in the preview image. So that way whichever specific heading is active, you're going to be seeing the image of that only specific list item. So there's that. I'm just going to be pasting in some other um list items as well just so that you can see. So we have here the entire screen getting filled. Obviously I can't really scroll. The reason for that is because the container has the height of 100 viewport height. So and then also overflow hidden. So it restricts me from scrolling. Now the next thing that we will be doing is um so first I'll just explain you the CSS. So for the font family we just have instrument serif uh and then georg as a fallback as well as serif and then some styling to the container list item. Um one thing to note here is that the list has the uh has display flex with flex direction as column and then the gap of that is 8 pixels. Now here the gap is pretty important. The reason for that is because um each of the individual list item would be placed 8 pixels away from the first one. So this 8 pixel doesn't really calculate or or get included in that specific element's height. So yeah, you just need to remember this because in JavaScript I'll be again um again basically telling you why this is important. So there's that and then with some styling to the list item. uh the last element doesn't have uh the border bottom some styling the index heading and then yeah um some styling for the active class for the heading as well as the index. So I can just show you in a bit. So here if I add the active class to the fifth one. So as you can see this has uh this basically translated along the x-axis a bit as well as the color for this changed to more towards white. So there is that. So yeah, that's basically what we need for the HTML as well as CSS. Now we'll start with the JavaScript. So the first thing that we do is we check for the window.load uh which is basically checks for all the elements getting loaded. So after everything is loaded up, you want to trigger or basically paste in the Linux code that I got from the documentation. So this basically binds your scroll trigger as well as desab with the animation and then basically gives you the smooth experience. So there's that and then we basically grab onto the different elements that we need. So we need the list list wrapper list items container and then the preview image. All right. And then we calculate the height that we need the elements to be able to scroll through. So since the container is going to get pinned, we need to be able to find the height to which the entire list would basically scroll to. So to do that we have the item height. So first we find the height of each individual item. So the way we do it is by using list items. We grab the first one and then we grab the offset height of that specific item. Offset height includes padding as well as border but this doesn't include the gap. So what we do is we add an extra eight to it just so that it gives it adds the gap of 8 pixels to the element itself. So the list item has this height. Now to get the height of the entire list or the entire list wrapper what we have to do is we have to multiply the list item with the number of items that we have. So we have list height time list items.length. But here you might have seen that I have subtracted one from the list items length. The reason for that is because when you start off the animation the first heading or the very top heading would always stay visible. The scroll portion is the portion that is not visible and that is excluding the first element. So this scroll this total scroll would be the scroll that you take into account excluding the very first one. So here we need to subtract one. Now to check the animation we basically add a gap tween which is gap.2 method. We target the list wrapper. We basically move the list wrapper towards the top with negative total scroll with no ease. Now when I save, let's refresh. You can see the container basically moves to the top. Now we just need to bind this with our animation with our scroll trigger. So we create the scroll trigger. The triggering point would be the container and this would start when the top of the container hits the top of the window. And then this would go on till we have scrolled till the total scroll that we just calculated. We want the container to stay pinned while we're scrolling through. And then we bind the animation with scrub of 0.8. So if you want to bind it, the initial or the usual value is one, which basically adds the animation with your scroll with no delay. But if you have 0.8, 8 you get this smooth sort of ease delay that makes it even smoother. So we have the scrub as 0.8 and then here we calculate the actual um update. Let's first save it. And now if I scroll you can see that the scrolling or basically the movement of the list list items is well bounded with our um scroll. Now the only thing that we have to do is as we scroll through the list item that is active we just need to change the class of that. So to do that on every update what we do is first we check for the self.program progress self.progress gives you a value between zero and one which is basically like how far you have scrolled through. If you're at the very top the scroll scroll progress would be zero and if you reach the very end it would be one. So you basically take that you multiply it with the number of elements that you have. Now here the reason for subtracting minus1 is because we need the index of element. Now obviously the index of elements start from zero and end till length minus one. So here we multiply that with the possible indexes that we can have and then we just round it up to get a more natural or a whole number value instead of having having decimal points. So let's just say you reach six you reach 6.2. So instead of showing you 6.2 2 since there's no specific index as 6.2 but there's always an index with six so it will round up and give you the numerical or the absolute value instead instead of the decimal points. So you just get the index based off the scroll position you're in and then you take that you run a for each to all the list items that you have. you add the class of active. So you use the toggle function and you add the class of active to the index that is equal to the active index. And now when I scroll the index that is active gets this active class and is white in color. Hopefully this makes sense. Now the next thing that we do is we grab onto the image of that active list item and then save that as as the preview image. So when I save and start scrolling you can see that the image of that active list item is what is visible here. Now, after you're done leaving the container at the very bottom, what we have to do is we have to remove or hide this preview image since we can't have this preview image show up for the entire users experience on the on the website. So, we need to remove this after we're done scrolling. So after I'm done scrolling, it basically hides itself. Also, we need to remove the active class from the list item. So we run again another for each. Um I could have only targeted the last one, but just in case. So I'm just going to be I'm just going to be running a for each and I'm going to be removing the active class from any list item if there's if there's multiple. So I'm just going to be removing the active class. So now if I reach the end, the animation is gone. Also this is not active anymore. So this is this is all good. But if I revisit the scroller or the container, you can see that now the image is no more visible because we've just hidden the image with opacity zero. So when you again revisit which is on enter back, you just bring the image back with this property. So yeah, this is it for this video. Hope it was fun. Um, I bring in cool stuff like this every week. So if you haven't subscribed the channel yet, make sure to subscribe. Turn the notifications bell on. Uh, we're really close to hitting 5,000 subscribers. So it would mean a lot if you guys could share this video with your with your fellow developer friends. Do comment down because commenting makes the video more engaging and basically shares it with to wider range of audiences. So yeah, that would really help me a lot. So yeah, that's it for this video. Meet you guys in the next. Till then, bye-bye. Take care and peace.

# Animation

Best selection of **Animation Website** examples for your inspiration...Read more

Animation is the rapid display of a sequence of images that create an illusion of movement. The effect is an optical illusion of motion created by the phenomenon of persistence of vision, and can be achieved in several ways, the most common being as a motion picture or video.

Animation has been about since before the web began, enhancing visual content and, in many cases, animation is used to highlight the interface. Today, animation is not just part of a websites content, it’s a fundamental feature, essential to increasing interaction with the site.

Since CSS3 was rolled out across most major browsers and devices, we
have witnessed an important evolution of animation techniques. In
addition to endless libraries for JavaScript-driven animations, the
panorama before us is truly spectacular; technologies for animated
content are branching out across the web thanks to Canvas, SVG, CSS3,
WebGL… The latest giant step is the unification of declarative and
imperative animations via web animation APIs to merge features from SVG
and CSS.

![Thumbnail (1920x1080)](https://i.ytimg.com/vi/IfSIOI9Qpb4/maxresdefault.jpg)
# [$10,000 AWWWARDS Animation — Built with GSAP + Next.js](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IfSIOI9Qpb4)

**Visibility**: Public
**Uploaded by**: [HankTheTank](http://www.youtube.com/@HankTheTankCoding)
**Uploaded at**: 2026-02-22T04:00:33-08:00
**Published at**: 2026-02-22T04:00:33-08:00
**Length**: 28:36
**Views**: 816
**Likes**: 60
**Category**: Science & Technology

## Description

```
This is a full Awwwards-style hero animation using Next.js 14 and GSAP — smooth staggered reveals, clip-path overlays, and responsive match media, all in one timeline.

✨ What we built:
• Staggered image gallery reveal
• Clip-path overlay wipe animation  
• Responsive hero scaling (mobile + desktop)
• Text reveal with overflow masking
• Dark overlay reveal transition
• GSAP matchMedia for breakpoint-aware animations

🔗 Links:

Live site → https://alexandra.dimaac.com/
Source code → https://github.com/Hank-D-Tank/alexandra
Inspiration (Alena Figurová) → https://aladesign.cz/

🛠 Stack: Next.js 14, GSAP, @gsap/react, Tailwind CSS

Drop a comment if you want a full breakdown! And hit subscribe for more frontend animation content.

#gsap #nextjs #webdesign #animation #frontend
```

## Endscreen

What separates a website you remember from the thousands you don’t? For me, it was the animations in an [Awwwards](https://www.awwwards.com/)-winning site, which made me feel like I wasn’t just browsing — I was part of its story. Each scroll was like turning the page of a storybook, each animation a small piece of magic. It wasn’t just a website; it was an experience. When these animations are done right, they become the heartbeat of a website’s design, elevating UX from functional to unforgettable.

Creating these experiences requires more than just aesthetic vision. Designers need to understand the technologies that bring animations to life and speak the language of developers. It’s not about becoming a coder — it’s about creating a deeper awareness beyond pixel-perfect Figma screens. This understanding helps designers collaborate more effectively with development teams, advocate for their ideas, and broaden their creative toolkit. With the right technical knowledge, designers can push boundaries, experiment boldly, and transform ambitious ideas into seamless, impactful experiences.

In this article, we’ll explore eight key animation techniques shaping modern web design and how designers can go beyond designing animations to implementing them for websites using easily available tools and libraries.

## **1\. Scroll Tracking & Smooth Scrolling**

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![Scroll-triggered animations revealing text and images on the C2MTL website, creating a seamless and engaging storytelling experience as users scroll.](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*eUNioiiz9d_FWIVnOLW8eg.gif)

[C2MTL](https://c2mtl.koki-kiko.com/)’s website is a great example of seamless scroll-triggered animations revealing text and images, enhancing user engagement through interactive storytelling.

Scroll tracking is a web development technique that monitors a user’s scroll position, direction, and speed on a webpage. This helps to create scroll-driven animations, where visual elements and effects are dynamically triggered and synchronized with the user’s scrolling. This enhances user engagement by making websites feel more interactive and responsive.

However, native browser scrolling can be inconsistent and jerky, especially with a mouse wheel. This can negatively impact scroll-triggered animations, causing them to appear unsmooth. Smooth scrolling libraries like [Lenis](https://lenis.darkroom.engineering/) or [Locomotive Scroll](https://github.com/locomotivemtl/locomotive-scroll) address this by overriding the default scrolling behavior, replacing it with a fluid, controlled motion. Combined with animation libraries like [GSAP](https://gsap.com/) and its ScrollTrigger plugin, they ensure animations are precisely synchronized with the user’s scroll, creating a polished and captivating experience.

Use Cases:

- **Animated Reveals:** Trigger animations (e.g., fades, slides, scaling) when elements enter the viewport, progressively disclosing content.
- **Parallax Scrolling:** Create a sense of depth by animating background elements at different speeds relative to the foreground.
- **Interactive Data Visualization:** Animate charts, graphs, or data points in response to scrolling, making complex information more engaging and understandable.

## **2\. Text-Splitting Animation**

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![Split text animation in the hero section of Motto’s website, dynamically revealing text to create an engaging visual experience.](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*pEcycoTGjjj_GegRgdD4Pg.gif)

[Motto](https://wearemotto.com/)’s website uses split text animation in the hero section at the top of each page, creating an engaging and dynamic text reveal.

Text-splitting animation is a technique where individual characters, words, or lines of text are treated as separate, animatable elements. Instead of animating a whole block of text as a single unit, each character or word can have its own independent animation, creating more dynamic and intricate visual effects.

This technique opens up a wide range of creative possibilities for text animation. It allows for effects that go beyond simple fades or movements of entire text blocks. By animating individual characters or words, you can create eye-catching effects like sequential reveals, cascading text, or playful letter interactions.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![Split text animation integrated across various sections of Sundae Creative’s website, creating a dynamic and engaging visual experience as users scroll.](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*TtEyg2V7o8EEreTG8VM1UQ.gif)

Split text animation is seamlessly integrated throughout various sections of [Sundae Creative](https://sundaecreative.com/en/home)’s website, delivering an engaging and dynamic visual experience as users scroll.

Use cases:

- **Hero Sections:** Make a strong first impression by animating the main headline on a website’s homepage with engaging character-by-character reveals or other dynamic effects.
- **Interactive Elements:** Enhance buttons, tooltips, or taglines with hover effects using split-text animation. Use text animations to respond to user scroll, hover, or clicks.
- **Emphasis and Highlights:** Draw attention to specific words or phrases within a paragraph by applying subtle animations like color changes, size variations, or slight movements.

Implementation:

- [**GSAP with SplitText plugin**](https://gsap.com/docs/v3/Plugins/SplitText/) **:** SplitText makes it easy to break apart the text in an HTML element so that each character, word, and/or line is in its own <div>, making complex animation simple.
- [**SplitType**](https://www.npmjs.com/package/split-type) **:** SplitType is a small javascript library that splits HTML text into elements so that lines, words, and characters can be animated independently. It was inspired by GSAP’s SplitText plugin, and can be used with any animation library.
- [**Splitting.js**](https://splitting.js.org/) **:** A popular and lightweight library specifically designed for splitting text into individual characters, words, or lines. It adds data attributes and CSS variables to the HTML, making it easy to target and animate the split elements.

## **3\. Micro-animations**

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![Micro-animations on the NEWPEACE website enhance the user experience with a captivating loading animation and animated illustrations in the hero header.](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*Mf1QLtcUoaoGVjl7k0ishw.gif)

Micro-animations are used on the [NEWPEACE](https://newpeace.jp/) website to create a captivating loading animation and bring illustrations in the hero header to life.

Micro-animations are small, subtle animations that provide visual feedback to user interactions. They are typically brief, functional, and often occur in response to a user action like clicking a button, hovering over an element, or submitting a form.

![An interactive button and animated footer on the Calm Craft website, created using Rive. I created an interactive button and a lively, animated [footer](https://rive.app/community/files/15899-29948-craft/) for the [Calm Craft](https://calmcraft.framer.website/) website using Rive, where each letterform reacts dynamically to cursor hovers, adding a playful touch. Micro-animations play a crucial role in enhancing usability and creating a more intuitive and engaging user interface. They can also guide users, clarify interactions, and add a touch of delight to the overall experience.

Use cases:

- **Interactive Buttons:** Change color, size, shadow, or add a subtle ripple effect when a button is hovered over or clicked, providing visual confirmation of the interaction.
- **Illustrations and Visual Elements:** Animated logos, icons, or illustrations in hero sections or onboarding flows.
- **Loading Indicators:** Creative loaders that visually represent progress while keeping users engaged during waiting times.
- **Icon Animations:** Add subtle movements or transformations to icons on hover or click, enhancing their visual appeal and interactivity.

I designed an interactive menu button for the [Calm Craft](https://calmcraft.framer.website/) website using Rive. Implementation:

- [**Rive**](https://rive.app/) **:** Rive is a real-time interactive design tool that allows you to design, animate, and integrate your creations directly into your websites, apps and games. It’s core strength lies in its State Machine feature. You can define different states for an element (e.g., “idle,” “hover,” “clicked”) and design how the animation transitions between these states based on user interactions.
- [**LottieFiles**](https://lottiefiles.com/) **:** Lottie is a JSON-based animation file format that brings animations created in Adobe After Effects (and other tools) to the web. Designers can create detailed micro-animations in After Effects, a powerful tool for motion graphics. These animations are then exported as Lottie JSON files using a plugin like Bodymovin.

## **4\. Transitions & reveals**

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![Page transitions on Metalab’s website, where clicking on a project image triggers a seamless zoom-in effect, smoothly transitioning to the next page.](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*GY1F1RghGE2dViAioMSRVQ.gif)

[Metalab](https://www.metalab.com/)’s website showcases beautifully crafted page transitions. When clicking on a project image, the image zooms in seamlessly, creating a smooth and engaging transition to the next page.

By transitions & reveals I refer to different creative ways users can move from one section, page, or state of a website to another. They serve as a bridge between content, ensuring that navigation feels smooth, engaging, and intuitive. These transitions can involve:

- Page transitions (e.g., fading out one page while fading in another).
- Section transitions within a single page (e.g., sliding, parallax effects).
- State changes for interactive elements (e.g., expanding menus, modal popups).

Creative transitions help maintain a cohesive and immersive experience, encouraging users to explore more of the site. They also add an element of delight, making the journey through a website as enjoyable as the destination.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![Page transitions on Alex Tkachev’s portfolio website, featuring a z-axis fade-out for the previous page and a seamless fade-in for the new page, creating a captivating and immersive experience.](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*bABhKPu6IX8p3qcvMGTSdQ.gif)

[Alex Tkachev’s portfolio website](https://alextkachev.com/) features stunning page transitions, where the previous page fades out along the z-axis, and the new page seamlessly fades in, creating a captivating experience.

Use cases:

- **Page Transitions:** Smoothly animate between two pages with effects like fades, slides, or wipes to avoid jarring content switches.
- **Content Reveal:** Sections of a page animate into view as the user scrolls, with effects like scaling, rotation, or parallax.
- **Menu Animations:** Sliding or morphing menus that enhance the experience of opening and closing navigation elements.
- **Storytelling:** Using animated sequences to guide users through a narrative, such as scrolling-triggered transitions that tell a story as the user navigates.

Implementation:

- [**GSAP**](https://gsap.com/) **:** A robust and highly powerful library for creating complex, [timeline-based animations](https://gsap.com/docs/v3/GSAP/Timeline). It offers fine-grained control over animation properties, easing, and sequencing. Often used with libraries like Barba.js or Highway for advanced page transition effects.
- [**barba.js**](https://barba.js.org/) **:** A widely used library that provides a structured way to implement page transitions. It handles prefetching content, updating the DOM, and offers hooks for integrating custom animations.
- [**Next.js**](https://nextjs.org/) and [**Framer Motion**](https://motion.dev/) are a powerful combination for creating smooth and visually stunning page transitions. Next.js handles the routing and server-side rendering efficiently, while Framer Motion, a React animation library, provides robust tools for creating animations with ease.

## **5\. Easing**

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![Toyfight’s website uses a variety of easing properties to enhance storytelling, with unique easing effects applied to page transitions, text, illustrations, and buttons, creating a cohesive and dynamic animation experience.](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*B9IpM4OKU6wxfj5iafPckA.gif)

[Toyfight’s](https://toyfight.co/) website masterfully leverages various easing properties to enhance storytelling. From page transitions to text, illustrations, and buttons, each element employs a unique easing effect, working in harmony to create a cohesive and dynamic animation experience.

Easing refers to the way an animation accelerates or decelerates over time, creating a more natural and realistic motion. Easing is what gives animations their “feel.” Without easing, animations would move at a constant, robotic speed. Easing functions add acceleration, deceleration, or other variations in speed, making animations feel more organic and lifelike.

Use Cases:

Essentially, any and all animations on the web can benefit from the application of appropriate easing functions to make it more refined and visually pleasing.

- **Micro-interactions:** Easing is essential for creating smooth and responsive micro-interactions, such as button hover effects, form input animations, and toggle transitions.
- **Page Transitions:** Easing functions can make page transitions feel more fluid and natural, avoiding abrupt changes in speed.
- **Scroll Animations:** When animating elements on scroll, easing can create a more engaging and dynamic experience.
- **Loading Animations:** Easing can make loading spinners or progress bars feel less monotonous and more visually interesting.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![GSAP’s Ease Visualizer showcasing a range of easing options to help users select and optimize animations for their websites.](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*u8GsioQZYWlO-0wL6kSNiQ.gif)

[GSAP’s Ease Visualizer](https://gsap.com/docs/v3/Eases/) is an excellent tool for exploring various easing options and selecting the perfect one to enhance animations on your website.

Implementation:

- **CSS Easing Functions:** Utilize CSS’s built-in easing functions (e.g., ease-in, ease-out, cubic-bezier) for transitions and animations to create smooth effects with minimal effort.
- **Custom Easing with JavaScript:** JavaScript libraries allow for more advanced easing effects with precise control.
- [**GSAP**](https://gsap.com/) **:** Offers a rich library of easing options like back, elastic, and bounce, with support for custom easing curves.
- [**Anime.js**](https://animejs.com/) **:** Provides predefined easings (easeInQuad, easeOutQuad) and allows custom cubic Bézier curves for precise control.
- [**Framer Motion**](https://motion.dev/) **:** Uses spring physics for natural animations but also supports standard easing curves for fine-tuned effects.

## **6\. SVG and Mask Animations**

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![Lightship’s website uses an SVG mask to dynamically reveal a video as the user scrolls, creating a visually striking and interactive experience.](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*IiJtcdnsuwLStuyN3Ga6iQ.gif)

[Lightship’s](https://lightshiprv.com/) website creatively uses an SVG mask to reveal a video as the user scrolls, delivering a visually striking and interactive experience.

SVG (Scalable Vector Graphics) and mask animations involve animating vector graphics and applying masks or clipping paths to reveal or hide elements dynamically. These techniques allow for highly scalable and lightweight animations, making them ideal for the web.

Use Cases:

SVG mask animations offer unique possibilities for creating sophisticated and intricate animation effects that are often difficult or impossible to achieve with traditional CSS or raster-based animations. They allow for:

- **Complex Shape Morphing:** Animate the mask’s shape to reveal or hide parts of the underlying SVG, creating the illusion of complex shape transformations.
- **Creative Reveals:** Use animated masks to progressively reveal or hide portions of an image, illustration, or text in a visually captivating way.
- **Text Effects:** Apply animated masks to text to create unique text reveals, write-on effects, or other dynamic typographic treatments.
- **Image Transitions:** Use animated masks to create smooth and visually interesting transitions between images.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![Accordion’s website utilizes masks for immersive page transitions and dynamic image carousels that contract and expand seamlessly, enhancing the overall user experience.](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*DzdeAVjWjTHLowJ-lOIXKA.gif)

[Accordion’s](https://www.accordion.net.au/) website uses masks not only to create immersive page transitions but also to design dynamic image carousels that seamlessly contract and expand, enhancing the user experience.

Implementation:

- **SMIL (Synchronized Multimedia Integration Language):** A declarative animation language that can be used within SVG elements. While SMIL support is declining in some browsers, it can still be useful for certain SVG animations. (Example above uses SMIL)
- **CSS Animations/Transitions:** You can animate the properties of the mask element or the masked element using CSS. This is generally the preferred approach for modern browsers.
- **JavaScript Animation Libraries:** Libraries like GSAP, Anime.js, or even plain JavaScript can be used to animate SVG elements and masks, providing more control and flexibility.

## **7\. 3D Animations**

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![rew District 24 e-commerce website features 3D renders of its beer cans, offering a visually captivating and interactive experience that highlights the product with stunning detail and creativity.](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*WY58b74Qdmxv-kA0mqC6kQ.gif)

[Brew District 24](https://brewdistrict24.com/en) e-commerce website uses 3D renders of its beer cans to create a visually captivating and interactive experience, showcasing the product with stunning detail and creativity.

3D animations on websites use three-dimensional elements to add depth, realism, and interactivity, creating more immersive and engaging user experiences. By simulating the real world or presenting creative visualizations, these animations allow brands and designers to captivate users, making websites stand out.

Use Cases:

- **Product Showcases:** E-commerce websites can use 3D models to allow customers to view products from all angles, zoom in on details, and even interact with them (e.g., rotate, open/close).
- **Interactive Learning:** Educational websites can use 3D models to create interactive learning experiences, such as exploring the human body, dissecting a machine, or visualizing complex scientific concepts.
- **Game-like Experiences:** 3D elements can be used to create immersive, game-like experiences on the web, blurring the lines between websites and interactive entertainment.
- **Data Visualization:** 3D can be used to visualize complex datasets in a more intuitive and understandable manner

![Mana’s Yerba Maté drink website features 3D renders of their cans, with dynamic animations where the can rotates, flips, and moves as users scroll, creating an immersive storytelling experience while keeping the product in focus.](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*A2AYkGKGj8kf3yq4u-Xghw.gif)

[Mana’s Yerba Maté](https://en.manayerbamate.com/) drink website utilizes 3D renders of their cans to deliver immersive and dynamic storytelling on the homepage. As users scroll, the can rotates, flips, and moves, keeping the experience engaging and the product consistently in focus.

Implementation:

- [**Spline**](https://spline.design/) **:** A highly user-friendly 3D design tool for creating interactive 3D scenes tailored for the web. Spline not only simplifies 3D design but also supports interactivity, allowing you to add event listeners and triggers to your 3D elements, making them dynamic and engaging.
- For more advanced asset creation, tools like [Blender](https://www.blender.org/) or [Cinema 4D](https://www.maxon.net/en/cinema-4d) are excellent choices for designing complex 3D models. Once the assets are ready, you can integrate them into your website using powerful libraries like Three.js for immersive 3D experiences.

## **8\. WebGL & Three.js**

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![Hatom’s website uses WebGL to deliver an immersive 3D experience, with interactive cursor movements that dynamically distort the content it hovers over, enhancing engagement and creating a memorable user experience.](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*yZ-qjtFm4gPHg9SWhEuFsA.gif)

[Hatom’s](https://www.hatom.com/) website leverages WebGL to create an immersive 3D experience. The movement of the cursor adds an interactive touch, distorting the content it hovers over, making the experience both engaging and memorable.

WebGL (Web Graphics Library) is a JavaScript API that enables rendering 2D and 3D graphics directly in the browser, leveraging GPU acceleration for smooth performance. Three.js, built on WebGL, simplifies creating immersive 3D environments and advanced visual effects. These technologies are ideal for creating dynamic, shader-based effects, interactive 3D galleries, and visually stunning animations that engage users deeply.

Use Cases:

- **3D Image Galleries:** Create interactive, rotating, or zoomable galleries for media-rich websites.
- **Shader Effects:** Apply unique visual effects, like water ripples, glass distortion, or particle systems, to website elements.
- **Interactive Backgrounds:** Add dynamic 3D environments or animated objects as website backgrounds.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![Anderson Mancini’s website employs WebGL and Three.js to create immersive 3D image carousels, offering a visually captivating and interactive browsing experience.](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*QMwMaz34ClpVC_3bjTdh2g.gif)

[Anderson Mancini’s](https://andersonmancini.dev/) website utilizes WebGL and Three.js to craft immersive 3D image carousels, delivering a visually captivating and interactive experience.

Implementation:

- [**​​Unicorn Studio**](https://www.unicorn.studio/) **:** A cutting-edge 3D web design and animation platform. It simplifies the creation of WebGL-based content with a no-code/low-code interface, enabling designers to build and customize immersive experiences without deep technical expertise. Unicorn Studio is particularly great for prototyping shaders, interactive galleries, and particle effects efficiently.
- [**Three.js**](https://threejs.org/) **:** Three.js simplifies many of the complexities of WebGL, providing a higher-level, more user-friendly API. It uses a scene graph to organize and manage 3D objects, making it easier to create complex scenes. It also provides built-in support for various materials, lighting models, and camera perspectives.
- [**Babylon.js**](https://www.babylonjs.com/) **:** It is another powerful and feature-rich JavaScript library for building 3D experiences with WebGL. It’s often used for games and other interactive 3D applications. It includes a built-in physics engine, making it easier to simulate realistic physics in your 3D scenes.

The animation techniques explored in this article are not a checklist but a toolkit — a set of powerful tools to draw from when designing dynamic and engaging websites. Not every technique will suit every project, and their use should always be guided by the website’s goals, audience, and core concept.

Equally important is considering the broader impact of these animations. When drawing from this toolkit, factors like accessibility, performance, and usability must remain at the forefront of every design decision. Animations should enhance the experience for all users, not create barriers or slow down the site. Striking the right balance between creativity and functionality is what separates great web design from the rest.
